questionList.questionTitle: Gauss law - what must be true when flux is zero
questionList.choiceQuestionContent: Which of the following statements is always true for a Gaussian surface through which the total electric flux is zero?

optionList.optionContent: There are no charged objects inside the surface.
The net charge enclosed by the surface is zero, but charged objects could still be inside the surface.
The electric field is zero everywhere on the surface.
At every location on the surface, the electric field is either zero, perpendicular to the surface, or parallel to the surface.

questionOption:
A .

There are no charged objects inside the surface.

B .

The net charge enclosed by the surface is zero, but charged objects could still be inside the surface.

C .

The electric field is zero everywhere on the surface.

D .

At every location on the surface, the electric field is either zero, perpendicular to the surface, or parallel to the surface.

questionList.questionTitle: Conductors - how is sphere charge -Q distributed
questionList.choiceQuestionContent: A solid conducting sphere has a net negative charge −Q. Which of the following correctly indicates how the excess charge is distributed, and provides a valid justification?

optionList.optionContent: The excess charge is distributed on the surface of the sphere, because negatively charged particles’ mutual repulsion causes them to move to the surface.
The excess charge is distributed on the surface of the sphere, because the positively charged particles move freely toward the sphere’s interior.
The net charge is distributed uniformly throughout the sphere, because negatively charged particles move randomly through the sphere, resulting in an equal distribution, on average, of the charge.

The net charge is distributed uniformly throughout the sphere, because negatively charged particles added to the sphere will arrange themselves in the configuration with the highest degree of symmetry.

questionOption:
A .

The excess charge is distributed on the surface of the sphere, because negatively charged particles’ mutual repulsion causes them to move to the surface.

B .

The excess charge is distributed on the surface of the sphere, because the positively charged particles move freely toward the sphere’s interior.

C .

The net charge is distributed uniformly throughout the sphere, because negatively charged particles move randomly through the sphere, resulting in an equal distribution, on average, of the charge.

D .

The net charge is distributed uniformly throughout the sphere, because negatively charged particles added to the sphere will arrange themselves in the configuration with the highest degree of symmetry.

questionList.questionTitle: Energy conservation - proton in 100 N-per-C field
questionList.choiceQuestionContent:
Two conducting plates hold equal and opposite charges that create a uniform electric field of magnitude E=100 N/C directed to the right, as shown. Points A and B are 0.0050 m apart. A proton is released from rest at Point A.

What is the kinetic energy of the proton when it reaches Point B ?

optionList.optionContent: Zero
8.0×10−20J
1.6×10−17J
3.2×10−15J

questionOption:
A .

Zero

B .

8.0×10−20J

C .

1.6×10−17J

D .

3.2×10−15J

questionList.questionTitle: B force on wires - currents doubled and reversed
questionList.choiceQuestionContent:
Two long, straight parallel wires X and Y are separated by a distance d and carry current I and 2I, as shown. The force on Wire X has magnitude F.

If the current in each wire is both doubled and reversed in direction, which of the following is true of the magnitude and direction of the new force on Wire X ?

  Magnitude Direction
A 2F Reversed
B 2F Unchanged
C 4F Reversed
D 4F Unchanged

optionList.optionContent: A
B
C
D

questionOption:
A .

A

B .

B

C .

C

D .

D

questionList.questionTitle: Electric potential E is quadratic function of x
questionList.choiceQuestionContent: The electric field along the x-axis varies with position x according to the equation E→(x)=a(3x2−x)i^, where a is a positive constant. Taking the electric potential to be zero at x=0, which expression is equal to the electric potential as a function of x?

optionList.optionContent: 6ax−a
−6ax+a
ax3−ax22
−ax3+ax22

questionOption:
A .

6ax−a

B .

−6ax+a

C .

ax3−ax22

D .

−ax3+ax22

questionList.questionTitle: Electromagnetism - force on loop entering B field
questionList.choiceQuestionContent:
A rectangular conducting loop is moving to the right at a constant speed as it enters a region of uniform magnetic field that is directed out of the plane of the figure, as shown. The top, right, bottom, and left segments of the loop are labeled Q , R , S, and T, respectively.

 

Which of the following correctly indicates the direction of the net magnetic force on the loop and provides a valid justification?

optionList.optionContent: To the left, because a leftward force is exerted on Segment R, no force is exerted on Segment T, and the forces exerted on Segment Q and Segment S are equal and opposite
To the left, because a leftward force is exerted on both Segment Q and Segment S, and no forces are exerted on Segment R and Segment T
No direction, because the velocity of the loop is constant and therefore no magnetic forces are exerted on any of the loop segments
No direction, because the forces exerted on opposite segments are equal and opposite and therefore the net force on the loop is zero

questionOption:
A .

To the left, because a leftward force is exerted on Segment R, no force is exerted on Segment T, and the forces exerted on Segment Q and Segment S are equal and opposite

B .

To the left, because a leftward force is exerted on both Segment Q and Segment S, and no forces are exerted on Segment R and Segment T

C .

No direction, because the velocity of the loop is constant and therefore no magnetic forces are exerted on any of the loop segments

D .

No direction, because the forces exerted on opposite segments are equal and opposite and therefore the net force on the loop is zero

questionList.questionTitle: Capacitors - speed vs t of particle between plates
questionList.choiceQuestionContent:
At time t=0, a small particle with an initial velocity v0 in the +x-direction enters the region between two charged capacitor plates, as shown. The particle has positive charge +Qp and mass Mp, and the electric field between the plates is directed in the + y -direction with a magnitude Ec. The particle passes through the region between the plates without contacting the plates.

If gravitational forces and capacitor edge effects are negligible, what is the particle’s speed as a function of time t during the time it is within the region between the plates?

optionList.optionContent: v0+(EcQpMp)t
v0+12(EcQpMp)t2
v02+(EcQptMp)2
v02+(12EcQpt2Mp)2

questionOption:
A .

v0+(EcQpMp)t

B .

v0+12(EcQpMp)t2

C .

v02+(EcQptMp)2

D .

v02+(12EcQpt2Mp)2

questionList.questionTitle: Electric PE - highest EPE of 3 charge systems
questionList.choiceQuestionContent:
Figures 1, 2, and 3 show three identical point charges with negative charge − Q in different arrangements. The distances between neighboring point charges, in terms of the distance d, are indicated.

Which of the following correctly compares the electric potential energies U 1 , U2, and U3 of the configurations in figures 1, 2, and 3, respectively?

optionList.optionContent: U1=U2=U3
U2>U1>U3
U3>U1>U2
(U2=U3)>U1

questionOption:
A .

U1=U2=U3

B .

U2>U1>U3

C .

U3>U1>U2

D .

(U2=U3)>U1

questionList.questionTitle: E field - conducting sphere charge from given E
questionList.choiceQuestionContent:
A solid conducting sphere has a radius of 0.060 m , as shown. The electric field at a distance of 0.090m from the center of the sphere is 2.0×105N/C toward the sphere.

If the electric field is due only to the excess charge of the sphere, what is the magnitude of the excess charge that is on the surface of the sphere?

optionList.optionContent: Zero
2.0×10−8C
8.0×10−8C
1.8×10−7C

questionOption:
A .

Zero

B .

2.0×10−8C

C .

8.0×10−8C

D .

1.8×10−7C

questionList.questionTitle: Magnetism - how to increase flux through square
questionList.choiceQuestionContent:
A region of space near the origin in an xyz-coordinate system has a uniform magnetic field B → in the + z -direction, as shown. A square loop lies in the xy-plane and is centered at the origin.

 

Which of the following changes will increase the magnitude of the magnetic flux through the loop?

optionList.optionContent: Rotating the square about the z-axis by 45 degrees.
Rotating the square about the x-axis by 45 degrees.
Increasing the magnitude of the magnetic field.
Rotating the magnetic field so that the field is in the positive +y-direction.

questionOption:
A .

Rotating the square about the z-axis by 45 degrees.

B .

Rotating the square about the x-axis by 45 degrees.

C .

Increasing the magnitude of the magnetic field.

D .

Rotating the magnetic field so that the field is in the positive +y-direction.

questionList.questionTitle: Coulombs law - sphere weight equals electric force
questionList.choiceQuestionContent:
Two nonconducting spheres have different radii R and R2, but the same mass M and the same uniform charge density ρ. The sphere of radius R2 is directly above but not touching the larger sphere, and its weight is balanced by the electrostatic force exerted by the larger sphere.

What is the distance between the sphere’s centers?

optionList.optionContent: ρR2πε0Mg
4ρR2πε0Mg
ρR33π2ε0Mg
8ρR33π2ε0Mg

questionOption:
A .

ρR2πε0Mg

B .

4ρR2πε0Mg

C .

ρR33π2ε0Mg

D .

8ρR33π2ε0Mg

questionList.questionTitle: 2-sphere potential set - signs of U-elec and W-ext
questionList.choiceQuestionContent:
Two small spheres, one with negative charge - Q and one with positive charge +Q, are fixed in place at the corners of an equilateral triangle and Point 1 is at the third corner. Point 2 is midway between the spheres as shown.

The electric potential energy of the two-sphere system shown in the figure is UELEC, and the work done by external forces to move the two spheres from infinitely far away to the locations shown in the figure is WEXT. Which of the following correctly identifies the signs of UELEC and WEXT?

UELECWEXT
ANegativeNegative
BNegativePositive
CPositiveNegative
DPositivePositive

optionList.optionContent: A
B
C
D

questionOption:
A .

A

B .

B

C .

C

D .

D

questionList.questionTitle: 2-sphere potential set - compare V at I and II
questionList.choiceQuestionContent:
Two small spheres, one with negative charge - Q and one with positive charge +Q, are fixed in place at the corners of an equilateral triangle and Point 1 is at the third corner. Point 2 is midway between the spheres as shown.

Which of the following correctly compares the relationship between the electric potentials V1 and V2 at points 1 and 2, respectively?

optionList.optionContent: V1=V2=0
(V1=V2)>0
V1<V2
V1>V2

questionOption:
A .

V1=V2=0

B .

(V1=V2)>0

C .

V1<V2

D .

V1>V2

questionList.questionTitle: EM induction - rod on rails with speed doubled
questionList.choiceQuestionContent:
A conducting rod of resistance R is in electrical contact with a U -shaped rail of width L and negligible resistance. The rod is pulled to the right at a constant speed v. A magnetic field B→ is directed into the page, as shown. The electric power dissipated by the rod is P.

 

What would the power dissipated by the rod be if the velocity of the rod were doubled?

optionList.optionContent: P4
P2
2P
4P

questionOption:
A .

P4

B .

P2

C .

2P

D .

4P

questionList.questionTitle: E field - E is zero midway between 2 point charges
questionList.choiceQuestionContent:
Two small stationary objects with unknown nonzero charges are isolated from all other charges. The electric field strength is zero at the midpoint of the line joining the two objects.

Which of the following statements about the charges of the objects must be true?

optionList.optionContent: The charges are equal in magnitude but opposite in sign.
The charges are equal in magnitude and have the same sign.
The charges are not necessarily equal in magnitude but have opposite signs.
The charges are not necessarily equal in magnitude but have the same sign.

questionOption:
A .

The charges are equal in magnitude but opposite in sign.

B .

The charges are equal in magnitude and have the same sign.

C .

The charges are not necessarily equal in magnitude but have opposite signs.

D .

The charges are not necessarily equal in magnitude but have the same sign.

questionList.questionTitle: Capacitors - 3 capacitors with slab removed from Z
questionList.choiceQuestionContent:
Three capacitors are connected in series, as shown in Figure 1 . The capacitors are identical except that capacitor X has air between its plates, whereas capacitors Y and Z each have a dielectric slab of dielectric constant κ>1 between their plates.

If the dielectric slab is removed from capacitor Z, as shown in Figure 2 , which of the following describes what will happen to ΔVX, ΔVY, and ΔVZ, the potential difference across capacitors X, Y, and Z, respectively?

  Potential difference across X Potential difference across Y Potential difference across Z
A Increases Increases Decreases
B Increases Decreases Increases
C Decreases Increases Decreases
D Decreases Decreases Increases

optionList.optionContent: A
B
C
D

questionOption:
A .

A

B .

B

C .

C

D .

D

questionList.questionTitle: B force on wires - wire A and 2 other wires
questionList.choiceQuestionContent:
The figure shows three long wires perpendicular to the plane of the figure and equidistant from each other. Two of the wires carry current into the plane, and the third carries current out of the plane. All three currents are equal in magnitude.

What is the direction of the net magnetic force on Wire A due to the other two wires?

optionList.optionContent: Toward the bottom of the figure
Toward the top of the figure
Toward the left
Toward the right

questionOption:
A .

Toward the bottom of the figure

B .

Toward the top of the figure

C .

Toward the left

D .

Toward the right

questionList.questionTitle: LR circuits - time constant with 2x inductance
questionList.choiceQuestionContent:
The figure shows a circuit with a battery of emf Ɛ, a resistor of resistance R , an inductor of inductance L, and an initially open switch. The switch is closed, and it takes a time Δt for the current in the circuit to reach approximately 63 % of its maximum value.

If the inductor is replaced with another inductor of inductance 2 L and the scenario is repeated, how long after closing the switch will the current reach approximately 63% of its maximum value?

optionList.optionContent: 4Δt
2Δt
Δt2
Δt4

questionOption:
A .

4Δt

B .

2Δt

C .

Δt2

D .

Δt4

questionList.questionTitle: Kirchhoff junction - find value of resistor X
questionList.choiceQuestionContent:
Three resistors are connected to an ideal battery with an emf Ɛ, as shown. Two of the resistors have resistances of R 0 and 2R0, as indicated in the diagram. The third resistor has unknown resistance X. The current in two of the branches is indicated.

What is the value of the unknown resistance X ?

optionList.optionContent: R04
R02
R0
5R0

questionOption:
A .

R04

B .

R02

C .

R0

D .

5R0

questionList.questionTitle: Electric PE - compare 3 two-sphere arrangements
questionList.choiceQuestionContent:
Three isolated arrangements of charged spheres are shown. The charges of the spheres and the distances between the spheres are indicated. Assume the electric potential energy is zero when the spheres are an infinite distance apart, and that the radii of the spheres are small compared to the distance between them.

Which of the following provides a correct ratio UA:UB:UC for the electric potential energies of arrangements A, B , and C, respectively?

optionList.optionContent: −2:2:1
2:2:1
−4:4:1
4:4:1

questionOption:
A .

−2:2:1

B .

2:2:1

C .

−4:4:1

D .

4:4:1

questionList.questionTitle: E field of distribution - rod half pos half neg
questionList.choiceQuestionContent:
A nonconducting rod of length 2D lies along the x-axis of an xy-coordinate plane with its center located at the origin, as shown. The left half of the rod has uniform negative linear charge density −λ and the right half of the rod has uniform positive linear charge density + λ .

Which integral expression could be solved to determine the x-component of the electric field at Point P, which is on the x-axis at position x = d ?

optionList.optionContent: −kλ∫0D dx(d−x)2+kλ∫−D0 dx(d−x)2
kλ∫0D dx(d−x)2−kλ∫−D0 dx(d−x)2
kλ∫−DD dx(d−x)2
2kλ∫0D dx(d−x)2

questionOption:
A .

−kλ∫0D dx(d−x)2+kλ∫−D0 dx(d−x)2

B .

kλ∫0D dx(d−x)2−kλ∫−D0 dx(d−x)2

C .

kλ∫−DD dx(d−x)2

D .

2kλ∫0D dx(d−x)2

questionList.questionTitle: LR circuits - steady state current for L and 2 Rs
questionList.choiceQuestionContent:
An inductor with inductance 4.0mH, a resistor with resistance 6.0kΩ, and a resistor with resistance 12 kΩ are connected to a battery with emf 6.0V and an initially open switch, as shown. The switch is then closed.

What is the current in the battery a long time after the switch has been closed?

optionList.optionContent: Zero
0.33mA
1.0mA
1.5mA

questionOption:
A .

Zero

B .

0.33mA

C .

1.0mA

D .

1.5mA

questionList.questionTitle: Charging process - rod sphere wire and ground
questionList.choiceQuestionContent:
A negatively charged rod is brought close to, but does not touch, an isolated, neutral conducting sphere, as shown. A grounded conducting wire is touched to the right side of the sphere and then removed. Finally, the rod is pulled away from the sphere.

Which of the following correctly indicates whether the final charge on the sphere is positive or negative, and provides a valid justification?

optionList.optionContent: Positive, because negative charge carriers have moved from the sphere to ground.
Positive, because positive charge carriers have moved from ground to the sphere.
Negative, because negative charge carriers have moved from ground to the sphere.
Negative, because positive charge carriers have moved from the sphere to ground.

questionOption:
A .

Positive, because negative charge carriers have moved from the sphere to ground.

B .

Positive, because positive charge carriers have moved from ground to the sphere.

C .

Negative, because negative charge carriers have moved from ground to the sphere.

D .

Negative, because positive charge carriers have moved from the sphere to ground.

questionList.questionTitle: B field of wires - two concentric loops
questionList.choiceQuestionContent:
Two concentric circular wire loops carry equal currents I in opposite directions, as shown. The inner loop has a radius a and carries a counterclockwise current. The outer loop has a radius b and carries a clockwise current.

What is the magnitude of the magnetic field at the center of the loops?

optionList.optionContent: μ0I2(1a−1b)
μ0I2(1a+1b)
μ0I2π(1a−1b)
μ0I2π(1a+1b)

questionOption:
A .

μ0I2(1a−1b)

B .

μ0I2(1a+1b)

C .

μ0I2π(1a−1b)

D .

μ0I2π(1a+1b)

questionList.questionTitle: Magnetism radius of proton path
questionList.choiceQuestionContent:
A proton with a velocity of 8.0×105m/s enters a region with a uniform magnetic field that is perpendicular to the proton’s initial velocity. After entering the field, the proton follows a circular path with a radius of 0.040 m .

What is the magnitude of the magnetic field?

optionList.optionContent: 3.3×10−20T
2.1×10−1T
1.7×105T
1.3×1026T

questionOption:
A .

3.3×10−20T

B .

2.1×10−1T

C .

1.7×105T

D .

1.3×1026T

questionList.questionTitle: Nonideal battery - voltmeter reading in RC circuit
questionList.choiceQuestionContent:
A resistor of resistance R and an uncharged capacitor of capacitance C are connected in a circuit with a nonideal battery that has emf Ɛ and internal resistance r. The circuit also contains switch S and an ideal voltmeter, as shown. The reading of the voltmeter is noted with the switch in the open position, and the switch is then closed.

How does the voltmeter reading immediately after the switch is closed compare to the reading before the switch is closed?

optionList.optionContent: It is higher.
It is lower.
It is the same and is zero.
It is the same and is not zero.

questionOption:
A .

It is higher.

B .

It is lower.

C .

It is the same and is zero.

D .

It is the same and is not zero.

questionList.questionTitle: Capacitors - 3 caps equivalent series-parallel
questionList.choiceQuestionContent:
Three identical capacitors with capacitance C are arranged as shown.

What is the equivalent capacitance between points A and B?

optionList.optionContent: 13C
23C
32C
3C

questionOption:
A .

13C

B .

23C

C .

32C

D .

3C

questionList.questionTitle: Capacitors - 40 micro-F capacitor energy at 2 mA
questionList.choiceQuestionContent:
A capacitor is initially uncharged when the switch is at position B in the circuit shown in the diagram. The switch is then moved to position A.

What is the energy stored by the capacitor when the current in the circuit is 2.0  mA ?

optionList.optionContent: 0.8mJ
1.2mJ
8.0mJ
18mJ

questionOption:
A .

0.8mJ

B .

1.2mJ

C .

8.0mJ

D .

18mJ

questionList.questionTitle: Electric potential can one get E from map of V
questionList.choiceQuestionContent: A student is given a diagram of equally spaced equipotential lines along with the values of the potentials for a region of space. Which of the following indicates whether it is possible for the student to create an electric field vector map in the same region of space and provides a valid justification?

optionList.optionContent: No, because electric field lines and equipotential lines are independent representations.
No, because the zero point for electric potential is not known it is not possible to uniquely determine the electric field.
Yes, because the electric field is everywhere directed at a right angle to an isoline, with a magnitude that is inversely proportional to the spacing between adjacent isolines.
Yes, because the electric field everywhere points along an isoline, with a magnitude that is directly related to the value of the potential on the isoline.

questionOption:
A .

No, because electric field lines and equipotential lines are independent representations.

B .

No, because the zero point for electric potential is not known it is not possible to uniquely determine the electric field.

C .

Yes, because the electric field is everywhere directed at a right angle to an isoline, with a magnitude that is inversely proportional to the spacing between adjacent isolines.

D .

Yes, because the electric field everywhere points along an isoline, with a magnitude that is directly related to the value of the potential on the isoline.

questionList.questionTitle: Electric current charge through wire
questionList.choiceQuestionContent: The current I in a wire is given as a function of time t by the equation I(t)=At3+B, where A and B are constants. Which expression is equal to the amount of charge that passes through a cross-sectional area of the wire from time t1 to t2?

optionList.optionContent: 3A(t22−t12)
A(t23−t13)+B
A(t2−t1)44+B(t2−t1)
A(t24−t14)4+B(t2−t1)

questionOption:
A .

3A(t22−t12)

B .

A(t23−t13)+B

C .

A(t2−t1)44+B(t2−t1)

D .

A(t24−t14)4+B(t2−t1)

questionList.choiceQuestionContent:
A charged sphere of radius R has uniform charge density ρ. The total charge of the sphere is Q tot .

How much charge would be enclosed by a Gaussian surface of radius R 2 centered on the center of the sphere?

Qtot4

Qtot16

A .

Qtot2

Qtot4

questionList.questionTitle: Electric flux curved and flat surfaces A1 and A2
questionList.choiceQuestionContent:
A point charge q is located at the origin of a coordinate system, as shown. Above the point charge are two surfaces, one curved and one flat. The curved surface has area A1=0.40 m2 and is a portion of a sphere of radius R that is centered at the origin. The flat surface is a circle of area A 2 = 0.30  m 2 . The two surfaces share the same circular border. The electric field at all points on the curved surface has magnitude E1=2.0 N/C and is directed away from the point charge. The electric field at the center of the flat surface has magnitude E2=8.0 N/C and is also directed away from the point charge.

What is the electric flux through the flat surface of area A 2 ?

optionList.optionContent: 0.60N·m2/C
0.80N·m2/C
1.5N·m2/C
2.4N·m2/C

questionOption:
A .

0.60N·m2/C

B .

0.80N·m2/C

C .

1.5N·m2/C

questionList.questionTitle: RC circuits - 3 resistors and 1 capacitor current
questionList.choiceQuestionContent:
The circuit shown consists of a battery of emf Ɛ, a switch, a capacitor of capacitance C , and three identical resistors each of resistance R. The switch is initially open, and the capacitor is uncharged. The switch is then closed.

What is the current in the battery when the circuit has reached a steady state after the switch is closed?

Ɛ3R
Ɛ2R
3ƐR

questionList.questionTitle: Magnetism compare B field for current slabs
questionList.choiceQuestionContent:
Three thin, conducting slabs in the xz-plane have uniform current densities directed in the − z -direction, as shown. The magnitude J of the current density and the thickness of each slab are indicated, as is the distance to a point labeled P from the middle of each slab. The widths of all three sheets along the x-axis are much greater than the distance to point P.

Which of the following correctly compares the magnitude of the magnetic fields B1, B 2 , and B3, at point P near slabs 1, 2, and 3, respectively?

optionList.optionContent: (B1=B2)>B3
(B2=B3)>B1
B3>B1>B2
B3>B2>B1

questionOption:
A .

(B1=B2)>B3

B .

(B2=B3)>B1

C .

B3>B1>B2

D .

B3>B2>B1

questionList.questionTitle: Electric flux net flux through Gaussian cube
questionList.choiceQuestionContent:
A cubic Gaussian surface with one corner located at the origin of a coordinate system has sides of length 0.1 m each extending along the +x, +y, and +z axes, as shown. This cube is in a region with a uniform electric field E described by the equation E = ( − 100 N/C ) i ∧ + ( − 50 N/C ) j ∧ + ( 200 N/C ) k ∧ .

What is the magnitude of the electric flux through the right (shaded) face of the Gaussian surface?

optionList.optionContent: Zero
0.5N/(m2·C)
1.0N/(m2·C)
2.5N/(m2·C)

A .

Zero

B .

0.5N/(m2·C)

C .

1.0N/(m2·C)

D .

2.5N/(m2·C)

questionList.questionTitle: Inductance - current when V is beta t-squared
questionList.choiceQuestionContent:
A variable voltage source is connected to an inductor of inductance L. The potential difference V of the source as a function of time t is given by the equation V(t)=βt2, where β is a positive constant. The current in the inductor at t=0 is zero.

What is the magnitude of the current in the inductor as a function of time?

optionList.optionContent: Zero
2βLt
βLt2
β3Lt3

Zero

B .

2βLt

questionList.questionTitle: LC circuits - what happens after switch is closed
questionList.choiceQuestionContent:
A circuit consists of a single loop with a charged capacitor, an inductor, and an initially open switch. The switch is then closed.

Which statement best describes the behavior of the circuit after the switch is closed?

optionList.optionContent: The inductor prevents the movement of charge carriers, so the capacitor remains charged.
The current in the inductor starts at a nonzero value and decreases at a constant rate.
The inductor current increases from zero and asymptotically approaches a constant maximum value.
The amount of net charge on each capacitor plate oscillates between a maximum amount of positive charge and a maximum amount of negative charge of equal magnitude.

questionOption:
A .

The inductor prevents the movement of charge carriers, so the capacitor remains charged.

B .

The current in the inductor starts at a nonzero value and decreases at a constant rate.

C .

The inductor current increases from zero and asymptotically approaches a constant maximum value.

D .

The amount of net charge on each capacitor plate oscillates between a maximum amount of positive charge and a maximum amount of negative charge of equal magnitude.

questionList.questionTitle: Equivalent resistance - six 2-ohm resistors
questionList.choiceQuestionContent:
Six identical resistors, each with resistance 2.0Ω, are connected to a 12 V battery, as shown.

What is the current in the battery?

optionList.optionContent: 1.0A
3.7A
4.0A
36A

questionOption:
A .

1.0A

B .

3.7A

C .

4.0A

D .

36A

questionList.questionTitle: Circuits replace resistor
questionList.choiceQuestionContent:
Two identical resistors, R1 and R2, each have resistance R . The resistors are connected in series with a battery. The potential difference across R1 is ΔV1. R2 is then replaced by different resistor with resistance 2R.

What is the potential difference across resistor R1 after R 2 is replaced?

optionList.optionContent: 13ΔV1
12ΔV1
23ΔV1
ΔV1

questionOption:
A .

13ΔV1

B .

12ΔV1

C .

23ΔV1

D .

ΔV1

questionList.questionTitle: Ohms law - find R from capacitor discharge data
questionList.choiceQuestionContent:
At time t1, the potential difference across a resistor in a circuit is Δ V 1 and the current in the resistor is I1. At a later time t2, the potential difference across the resistor is ΔV2 and the current in the resistor is I2.

Which expression is equal to the resistance of the resistor?

optionList.optionContent: ΔV1−ΔV2I1−I2
ΔV1I1−ΔV2I2
I1−I2ΔV1−ΔV2
I1ΔV1−I2ΔV2

questionOption:
A .

ΔV1−ΔV2I1−I2

B .

ΔV1I1−ΔV2I2

C .

I1−I2ΔV1−ΔV2

D .

I1ΔV1−I2ΔV2

questionList.questionTitle: Metal Bar on Rails mathematical reasoning

subjectiveQuestionList.questionContent:
Show all your work for each part of the question. The parts within the question may not have equal weight.

 

A metal bar of mass M can slide with negligible friction on a pair of parallel rails that are inclined at an angle θ from the horizontal, as shown in Figure 1. There is a uniform magnetic field B→ perpendicular to the plane formed by the rails, which are separated by a distance D. A circuit is formed by the bar, the rails, and a resistor of resistance R connected across the top ends of the two rails. The bar and the rails have negligible resistance and are in electrical contact with each other.

In the following, take the positive direction to be parallel to the rails down the incline, and positive current in the circuit to be counterclockwise when viewed from above.

(a)

  • i. On the axes in Figure 2, sketch a graph of the current I induced in the circuit as a function of the component of the velocity of the bar parallel to the incline. Your sketch should include positive and negative values of v.

• ii. Derive an expression for the net force Fnet exerted on the bar when the bar is moving with a nonzero speed down the rails. Express your answer in terms of M, θ, B, D, R, v, and physical constants, as appropriate. Begin your derivation by writing a fundamental physics principle or an equation from the reference booklet.

(b) The rails are repositioned to be horizontal, as shown in Figure 3, with the magnetic field still perpendicular to the plane formed by the rails. The bar is then given an initial velocity v0 in the positive direction, which is now toward the right.

  • i. Using the expression for Fnet from part (a)(ii), derive, but do NOT solve, a differential equation for the bar’s velocity v when the rails are horizontal. Express your equation in terms of v and time t, as well as M, θ, B, D, R, and physical constants, as appropriate. Begin your derivation by writing a fundamental physics principle or an equation from the reference booklet.
• ii. Solve the differential equation from part (b)(i) to derive an expression for the speed v of the bar as a function of time t.

questionList.questionTitle: Three charged particles TBR

subjectiveQuestionList.questionContent:
Show all your work for each part of the question. The parts within the question may not have equal weight.

 

Three charged particles, each of mass M, are arranged in an xy-coordinate plane at the positions shown in Figure 1. Particle 1 has a net negative charge −Q and particles 2 and 3 each have a net positive charge +Q. Particles 2 and 3 are fixed in place and Particle 1 is held in place by a force FH. Particles 2 and 3 remain fixed in place while Particle 1 is then released from rest and sometime later is instantaneously located at the origin (0,0). Neglect gravitational forces.

(a) The following dots represent Particle 1 at two positions: fixed in place at its initial position (−D,0) before being released, and at position (0,0) sometime after being released. On each dot, draw and label the forces (not components) exerted on Particle 1 at the indicated position. Each force must be represented by a distinct arrow starting on, and pointing away from, the appropriate dot.

(b) Derive an expression for the magnitude of the acceleration of Particle 1 immediately after it is released at (−D,0) in terms of D, M, Q, and physical constants, as appropriate. If you need to draw anything other than what you have shown in part (a) to assist in your solution, use the space below. Do NOT add anything to Figure 1.

(c) On the grids provided, sketch graphs of the electric potential energy U (Figure 3) and kinetic energy K (Figure 4) of the three-particle system as a function of Particle 1’s position x along the x-axis between the points x=−D and x=+D. Take the potential energy U of the system to be zero when Particle 1 is at x=0.

(d) For the graph of U(x) you sketched in Figure 3, indicate whether the graph is or is not consistent with the diagram you drew in part (a) when the particle is at position (0,0). Briefly justify your answer.

questionList.questionTitle: Resistor experiments

subjectiveQuestionList.questionContent:
Show all your work for each part of the question. The parts within the question may not have equal weight.


Students are given a cylindrical resistor and asked to determine if the resistor is ohmic.

(a) Describe an experimental procedure to collect data that would allow the students to use a graph to determine whether the resistor is ohmic. Include any steps necessary to reduce experimental uncertainty.
(b) Describe how the data could be analyzed to determine whether the resistor is ohmic.

The students are given several cylindrical resistors that are made from the same material and are known to be ohmic. The students are asked to determine the resistivity ρ1 of the material that the resistors are made from. The resistors have various cross-sectional areas A and lengths L, as shown. They connect each resistor, one at a time, to a circuit in which an ammeter measures the current in the resistor and a variable power supply is set so that the potential difference across the cylinder is 5.0V. The students’ data are listed in the table.

A(m2)L(m)I(mA)
1.20×10−60.0204.5
1.20×10−60.0402.5
4.40×10−60.0408.0
4.40×10−60.0605.3
9.60×10−60.06012.5

(c)

  • i. Indicate what measured and/or calculated quantities could be graphed to produce a straight line that could be used to calculate an experimental value of the resistivity ρ1. Use the blank columns in the table to list any calculated quantities you will graph other than the data provided.

    Horizontal axis: __________          Vertical axis: __________
• ii. Plot the data points for the quantities indicated in part (c)(i) on the grid provided. Clearly scale and label all axes, including units, as appropriate.
• iii. Draw a best-fit line for the data graphed in part (c)(ii).
(d) Using the best-fit line and the potential difference of 5.0V, calculate an experimental value of the resistivity ρ1.

  • i. Indicate what measured and/or calculated quantities could be graphed to produce a straight line that could be used to calculate an experimental value of the resistivity ρ1. Use the blank columns in the table to list any calculated quantities you will graph other than the data provided.

    Horizontal axis: __________          Vertical axis: __________
• ii. Plot the data points for the quantities indicated in part (c)(i) on the grid provided. Clearly scale and label all axes, including units, as appropriate.
• iii. Draw a best-fit line for the data graphed in part (c)(ii).
(d) Using the best-fit line and the potential difference of 5.0V, calculate an experimental value of the resistivity ρ1.

questionList.questionTitle: Comparing magnetic fields of solenoids QQT

subjectiveQuestionList.questionContent:
Show all your work for each part of the question. The parts within the question may not have equal weight.

 

A long solenoid has current I0, as shown in Figure 1 . The solenoid has length L, radius R, and N turns of conducting wire. At point P1, which is located at the center of the solenoid along the central axis, the magnetic field has magnitude B1.

A second solenoid has the same length, radius, and current as the original solenoid, but 2N turns of wire (not shown). For the second solenoid, the magnetic field has magnitude B2 at point P 2 , which is located at the center of the central axis of the second solenoid.

(a) Indicate whether B2 is greater than, less than, or equal to B 1 .

_____ B2>B1     _____B2<B1     _____ B 2 = B 1

Justify your answer without manipulating equations.

(b) Using Ampère’s law, derive an expression for the magnitude B1 of the magnetic field at point P1 in terms of I0, L, R, N, and physical constants, as appropriate. Begin your derivation by writing a fundamental physics principle or an equation from the reference booklet.
(c) Does your equation in part (b) agree with your reasoning in part (a)? Briefly justify your answer.

partList.directionsText:
PHYSICS C: ELECTRICITY AND MAGNETISM
SECTION I
Time—45 minutes
35 Questions
Directions: Each of the questions or incomplete statements below is followed by five suggested answers or completions. Select the one that is best in each case and then fill in the corresponding circle on the answer sheet.

PHYSICS C: ELECTRICITY AND MAGNETTSM
SECTION II
Time—45 minutes
3 Questions
Directions: Answer all three questions. The suggested time is sbout 15 minutes for answering each of the questions, which are worth 15 points each. The parts within a question may not have equal weight. Show all your work in this booklet in the spaces provided after each part.
